title: Economists douse inflation hopes
full_text:
Treasurer Jim Chalmers' optimism over progress in taming inflation since Labor's May budget has been tempered by leading economists who say the numbers are still worryingly high and trending in the wrong direction.
While Australia had dodged an economic bullet with the oil price hitting a four-month low after the ceasefire in the Middle East, economists said fuel costs would not immediately bounce back to pre-war levels as governments around the world replenished their stockpiles.
Chalmers told the ABC's Insiders program on Sunday that Treasury was now forecasting headline inflation to reach 4.25 per cent by June, down from the 5 per cent figure in May's budget. He said Labor had made "very welcome progress" on inflation that had been underreported by the media.
"Relative to previous expectations, we have made some progress. Some were expecting inflation would be a lot worse," agreed AMP economist My Bui, but she described the current level as still "quite worrying".
"Have we made significant long-term progress? I would say no, given inflation in the housing sector and our stagnant productivity growth rate."
Taming inflation has been a long-running political and economic headache for the Albanese government. Inflation has not been sustainably within the Reserve Bank's 2 per cent to 3 per cent target range for five years, contributing to record low consumer confidence and a cost-of-living crisis.
Due to the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and fears of a global growth slowdown, markets are now pricing in a 41 per cent chance of another 25 basis point rate rise by the RBA this year.
But a string of data from the Australian Bureau of Statistics last week fuelled concerns that the economy may not be slowing enough to ease price rises. The unemployment rate dropped to 4.4 per cent in May, while household spending jumped higher than expected.
KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne said Chalmers was citing Treasury's headline inflation rate forecast, which he said was a "virtually useless" statistic because Labor's fuel excise cut artificially reduced the rate.
"Underlying inflation is more important right now and we're still seeing that rise. It is 1.1 percentage points above the RBA's target of 2.5 per cent," Rynne said.
"We are a long way from saying inflation is back under control. It is still going in the wrong direction."
The RBA's preferred measure of underlying inflation, which strips out the most volatile items, rose from 3.4 per cent to 3.6 per cent in May.
The government halved the fuel excise rate for three months from April, which reduced fuel costs and therefore headline inflation. The measure was extended for another month beyond July, but at a lower rate.
"The government is still running a fiscal policy that is at odds with what monetary policy is trying to achieve in bringing inflation down," Rynne said.
Challenger chief economist Jonathan Kearns attributed Treasury's new inflation forecast to a lower oil price in the month following the May 12 budget, which preceded the announcement of a fragile 60-day ceasefire between Iran and the US, and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.
The budget assumed the Tapis oil price would average about $US100 over the fourth quarter of 2026, before declining to $US80 by June 30, 2027.
Brent crude is well ahead of that schedule, reaching $US73 on Monday.
"There was a huge amount of uncertainty on the evolution of the war in the Middle East. Most of the improvement is just the resolution of the conflict, which means oil prices aren't going as high as feared," Kearns said.
However, he said the oil price would not immediately return to its pre-conflict level due to the destruction of oil infrastructure in the Gulf and governments topping up their depleted fuel stockpiles.
Independent economist Chris Richardson said avoiding the 5 per cent inflation forecast was good news for the economy and Australian consumers, but it could be bad news for Chalmers' budget.
"The treasurer is in effect saying that we dodged a bullet, or at least the bullet isn't as bad as it might have been," Richardson said.
"What that means is broadly good news for the economy, good news for interest rate risks and bad news for the budget."
Richardson estimated that lower inflation would cost the budget around $1 billion in 2025-26 due to lower-than-forecast revenue from income taxes, which would be partially offset by lower government spending.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Banking and Economy
